name: Khubayb ibn Adiy
title: Khubayb ibn Adiy (RA): A Prisoner of Many Miracles
meta_description: The story of Khubayb ibn Adiy (RA), the companion captured at al-Raji and held for execution in Makkah. A prisoner who refused to wrong anyone, and met death with a steady heart.
keywords: Khubayb ibn Adiy, story of Khubayb, al-Raji, sahaba stories, the firsts, companions of the Prophet, prayer before death
lede: He was a prisoner in Makkah, alone, marked for execution, with no friend left alive to stand beside him. He could have saved himself with a single word, or a single act of cruelty. He chose neither.

This is the story of Khubayb ibn Adiy (may Allah be pleased with him): a companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ whose captivity carried more miracles than almost any other, and whose last hours became a lesson the believers would never stop repeating.

paras: Khubayb came from the tribe of Banu Aws among the Ansar of Madinah. He belonged to those original helpers who received the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ when he arrived, and who gave him their support without holding anything back.

He was also one of the people of Badr. That alone was a lofty title among the companions. He had stayed the course with the Prophet ﷺ through the hardest days, the kind of man who was there at the beginning and did not waver.

His name itself was gentle. In Arabic it is the smaller form of an older name, almost like saying "little." There is a quiet irony in that, because the test placed on this man would be anything but small.

His life belongs to a long line of believers who were persecuted simply for standing with the truth. The same spirit that carried Bilal and Khabbab through their torture in Makkah would carry Khubayb through his. What follows is one of the most painful chapters in the whole story, and also one of the most luminous.

Khubayb was sent out with a small group of companions, and at a place called al-Raji they were ambushed. It was a betrayal against every custom of the time: unsuspecting men, surrounded, with no fair fight offered to them. It is among the least remembered and most painful episodes in the life of the Prophet ﷺ.

The captors called down to the trapped men with assurances. We do not want to harm you, they said. We only want to take you to Makkah and gain something for you. Three of the companions did not trust it and chose to fight where they stood, and they were killed. Three others came down.

The moment they descended, they were seized and chained with a roughness that gave the promise away. One of the three, seeing the deception for what it was, took up his sword again and was killed for it. That left two prisoners to be carried to Makkah, Khubayb among them.

Both men had fought at Badr, and both had a price on their heads. The families who had lost their leaders that day were waiting, and they meant to buy these prisoners and take their revenge. Khubayb was sold into the hands of one such family and marked for death.

sections.h2: The first prisoner to ask for halal food
paras: The captors in Makkah held a strange sense of sanctity. They would not kill in a sacred month, so they kept Khubayb a prisoner until the month of Safar, roughly a month away. He knew the date of his own death, and he knew it was coming.

The woman set to watch him was Zaynab bint al-Harith, not yet a believer. Her own father had been among those Khubayb fought at Badr, so she had no reason to think kindly of him. Yet she would later narrate his story in full, and the scholars say no companion has more miracles reported about him than Khubayb.

The first was the simplest, and the most telling. When she asked if he had any request, he said only this: do not bring me meat slaughtered in the name of your idols. He would rather starve than eat it.

Think of what that means. A man counting down the days to his own execution, in chains, at the mercy of his enemies, and the thing he guarded most carefully was his obedience to Allah. The danger to his body did not loosen his hold on his faith.

Then Zaynab saw something she could not explain. Khubayb sat in his cell with a bunch of grapes, and she said they were larger than any she had ever seen. There were no grapes growing anywhere in Makkah at that time, and she was giving him nothing of the kind.

She came back, and the grapes were there again. And again, and again. It unsettled her at first, this fruit that came from nowhere. Finally she asked him where it came from, since it was not from her hand.

He answered that his Lord was feeding him. The provision came from Allah alone, given to a man his captors were starving toward death. It is the same answer recorded long before, when the provision of another righteous servant puzzled the one who watched over her:

ayah.text: Her Lord graciously accepted her and made her grow in goodness, and entrusted her to the charge of Zachariah. Whenever Zachariah went in to see her in her sanctuary, he found her supplied with provisions. He said, 'Mary, how is it you have these provisions?' and she said, 'They are from God: God provides limitlessly for whoever He will.'

paras: As the day drew near, Khubayb asked Zaynab for a razor. She asked why. He said he wished to groom himself for his meeting with his Lord. She gave it to him.

A while later she lost sight of her small child, and her heart dropped. She went looking and found the boy sitting on Khubayb's lap, the razor still in his hand. Here was the moment any captor would have feared, and any prisoner might have used: a hostage, a blade, a way out.

He looked at her and understood her terror. He said, in effect, you think I would kill him? We the followers of Muhammad ﷺ are not like that. He set the razor down and handed the child back. He had been betrayed and chained and condemned, and still he would not let himself become a man who wrongs the innocent.

It is the heart of who he was. A person who is wronged often feels entitled to wrong someone in return, and tells himself the injustice he suffered has earned him the right. Khubayb refused that bargain even with his life on the line. Being a Muslim, he showed, means you do not become an oppressor just because you have been oppressed.

sections.h2: Two units of prayer, and a steady heart
paras: When the month of Safar came, they took him outside the sacred precinct to a place called al-Tanim, the nearest point beyond the Haram. There they prepared to put him to death in the open, as an example to anyone watching. They told him to make a last wish, and he asked to pray two units of prayer first.

He prayed quickly, then turned to them and explained why. If he had not feared they would think he was delaying out of fear of death, he said, he would have prayed much longer. He did not want to give them that satisfaction. With this he set a practice that captured believers after him would follow: two units of prayer before death.

Then they began to break him slowly. They asked whether he would rather be safe at home, with the Prophet ﷺ put in his place instead. Barely able to speak, he said he would not wish even a thorn to prick the Prophet ﷺ to spare himself all of this. Even Abu Sufyan, who was watching, admitted he had never seen anyone love another the way the companions loved the Prophet ﷺ.

As the torture went on, Khubayb began to speak in verse. He described the crowds gathered against him, the women and children brought to watch, and turned his grief to his Lord. He said he wept, but not from fear of death, for he knew he had to die in any case. What he feared was the Fire. And then the line the believers would carry after him: I do not care how I am killed, so long as I die a Muslim, and I will show my enemy no fear, for my return is to my Lord. It was, in its full sense, the first crucifixion in the history of Islam, and yet the man on the stake was not the one who seemed defeated.

sections.h2: A salaam with no one to carry it
paras: As his strength left him, Khubayb realized he would not see the Prophet ﷺ again in this life. So he called out to Allah, in front of all of them, that there was no one present to carry his salaam to the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. He asked Allah Himself to deliver it.

Far away in Madinah, the Prophet ﷺ was overcome in the way he was when revelation came, and told his companions that Khubayb had sent his greeting. He returned the salaam. He grieved deeply for the companions who had been lost at al-Raji, and for a month afterward, in every prayer, he prayed for them.

Khubayb was killed at al-Tanim, where pilgrims today renew their intention to return to the sacred house. His captors tried to turn his face away from the qiblah, and each time he turned it back. When they left his body, they found it had turned toward the qiblah on its own, and they understood that they had killed no ordinary man.

Later the Prophet ﷺ sent two trusted men on a quiet mission to bring the body back. Though a long time had passed, it had not decayed. Khubayb was carried home to Madinah and buried among his people, the helper who had given everything he had.

lessons_intro: His story is short, and almost all of it takes place in chains. Yet it holds a kind of strength that ease rarely produces.

lessons.bold: Do not let being wronged make you a wrongdoer.
lessons.text: Khubayb was betrayed, chained, and condemned, and still he refused to harm a child to save himself. Being treated unjustly never gave him licence to be unjust.

lessons.bold: Trust that provision comes from Allah.
lessons.text: He guarded what he ate even as a starving prisoner, and Allah fed him from where no one could account for it. The heart that holds to its principles is never truly abandoned.

lessons.bold: Meet hardship without giving fear the last word.
lessons.text: He kept his final prayer short so his enemies would not mistake devotion for dread. His certainty stayed visible to the very end.

lessons.bold: Love what is good more than you fear loss.
lessons.text: He would not wish a single thorn upon the Prophet ﷺ to spare himself. His love was larger than his own pain.

faq.a: A companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ from the Ansar of Madinah and one of the people of Badr. He was captured in the ambush at al-Raji and held as a prisoner in Makkah, where he was eventually executed.

A small group of companions was ambushed in betrayal at a place called al-Raji. Several were killed on the spot. Khubayb and another companion were taken as prisoners to Makkah, where families who had lost men at Badr sought revenge on them.

faq.q: Why are there so many miracles in his story?
faq.a: His captor, Zaynab bint al-Harith, later narrated what she witnessed: provisions appearing out of season, his calm and his mercy, and his body turning toward the qiblah. The scholars say no companion has more miracles reported about him than Khubayb.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Khubayb?
faq.a: That being wronged is no excuse to wrong others, that provision and protection come from Allah, and that faith can stay steady and even gentle in the hardest moment a person can face.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Most of us will never be tested the way Khubayb was. But the choice at the centre of his story is one we meet often, in much smaller forms. When we are treated unfairly, do we hold to what is right, or do we tell ourselves that the wrong done to us has earned us the right to do wrong back?

Khubayb answers that question with a razor set down and a child handed back. He shows that a person can be stripped of everything and still keep the one thing that matters: a heart that refuses to harm, that trusts Allah for its provision, and that meets even its last hour without letting fear speak for it. Carry a little of that steadiness into your own day, and his story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Khubayb ibn Adiy (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). The Quran translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Quran 3:37). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
He was a prisoner in Makkah.

Alone. Marked for execution. No friend left alive beside him.

He could have saved himself with one word. He chose not to.

Khubayb ibn Adiy (RA) was one of the early helpers of Madinah, and one of the people of Badr.

He stayed with the Prophet ﷺ through the hardest days, and did not waver.

Captured in an ambush, held for execution, his first request to his guard was simple.

Do not bring me meat slaughtered for your idols. He would rather starve.

He was counting down to death, and still he guarded his obedience to Allah.

His guard kept finding him with grapes the size of nothing she had ever seen.

There were no grapes in Makkah that season. She gave him none.

"My Lord feeds me," he said.

She lost sight of her child, then found the boy on Khubayb's lap, a razor in his hand.

Any prisoner would have used the moment.

"You think I would kill him? We the followers of Muhammad ﷺ are not like that."

Before they killed him, they let him pray.

He prayed two short units, then said: I did not want you to think I was delaying out of fear of death.

He set a practice the believers would follow for centuries.

They asked if he would rather be home, with the Prophet ﷺ in his place.

Barely able to speak, he said he would not wish a single thorn to prick the Prophet ﷺ to spare himself any of it.

**Do not let being wronged make you a wrongdoer.**

Betrayed, chained, condemned, he still would not harm an innocent to save himself.

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
Khubayb ibn Adiy (may Allah be pleased with him) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, captured in betrayal and held in Makkah for execution. Alone, with no friend left alive beside him, he had every reason to break.

Instead he refused meat slaughtered for idols even as a starving prisoner. He set down a razor and handed back a captor's child when he could have used the boy to bargain for his life. And before they killed him, he kept his last prayer short so no one would mistake his devotion for fear.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/khubayb-ibn-addiy

email.subject: The prisoner who would not become a wrongdoer
email.alt_subject: He could have saved himself. He chose not to.
email.preview: Alone in Makkah, marked for death, Khubayb still refused to harm an innocent to escape.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Khubayb ibn Adiy (may Allah be pleased with him) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, one of the early helpers of Madinah and one of the people of Badr.

He was sent out with a small group and ambushed in betrayal at a place called al-Raji. Several of his companions were killed. Khubayb was taken to Makkah as a prisoner, bought by a family who had lost men at Badr and marked him for execution.

The woman set to watch him, not yet a believer, would later narrate what she saw. The scholars say no companion has more miracles reported about him than Khubayb.

The first was the simplest. When she asked if he had any request, he said only this: do not bring me meat slaughtered in the name of your idols. A man counting down to his own death, and what he guarded most carefully was his obedience to Allah.

Then she began finding him with grapes out of season, when none grew anywhere in Makkah and she was giving him none. "My Lord feeds me," he told her.

One day she lost sight of her small child and found the boy sitting on Khubayb's lap, a razor in his hand. Any prisoner would have used the moment to bargain for his life. He set the razor down and handed the child back. "You think I would kill him? We the followers of Muhammad ﷺ are not like that."

There is a lesson in that scene that is easy to miss. He had been betrayed, chained, and condemned, and still he refused to let himself become a man who wrongs the innocent. Being treated unjustly never gave him licence to be unjust.

When they finally took him out to be killed, he asked to pray two units first, and kept them short so his enemies would not mistake his calm for fear. He met the worst hour a person can face with a steady heart.

May Allah be pleased with him, and give us a measure of that steadiness when we are wronged.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: Khubayb ibn Adiy (may Allah be pleased with him) was one of the early helpers of Madinah, a man who received the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ at the beginning and stood by him through the hardest days. He was one of the people of Badr.

He was sent out with a small group of companions, and at a place called al-Raji they were ambushed in betrayal. Some were killed where they stood. Khubayb was seized and carried to Makkah, sold to a family who had lost men at Badr and who marked him for execution.

The woman set to watch him was not yet a believer, and she would spend the rest of her life telling what she saw. When she asked if he had any request, he said only that he would not eat meat slaughtered for idols. He would rather starve. A man counting down to death, still guarding his obedience to Allah.
Then she began to find him with grapes, large and fresh, when no grapes grew anywhere in Makkah and she had given him none. She asked where they came from. "My Lord feeds me," he said.

One day she lost sight of her small child and found the boy on Khubayb's lap, a razor in his hand. Her heart dropped. But he only looked at her and said, you think I would kill him? We the followers of Muhammad ﷺ are not like that. He set the razor down and handed the child back. Betrayed and chained and condemned, he still would not harm the innocent.

When they took him out to be killed, he asked to pray two units first, and kept them short so his enemies would not mistake his calm for fear. They asked if he would rather be safe at home with the Prophet ﷺ in his place. He said he would not wish even a thorn to prick the Prophet ﷺ to spare himself any of it.

As his strength left him, he asked Allah to carry his salaam to the Prophet ﷺ, since no one present would. Far away in Madinah, the Prophet ﷺ received it and returned it. When his captors left his body, they found it had turned toward the qiblah on its own.
storybook.pullquote: You think I would kill him? We the followers of Muhammad ﷺ are not like that.
storybook.reflection: Khubayb was stripped of his freedom, his safety, and finally his life, and still he kept the one thing no one could take: a heart that refused to harm. When we are wronged, the easy path is to tell ourselves it has earned us the right to wrong someone back. His story quietly closes that door. Hold to what is right, especially when you have every excuse not to.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
